coredata.dc:title: Excitation dependent emission combined with different quenching manners supports carbon dots to achieve multi-mode sensing
coredata.dc:creator: Chun Li,
Liu, Weijian
Sun, Xiaobo
Pan, Wei
Yu, Guifeng
Wang, Jinping
coredata.dc:description: In this paper, multi-mode sensing for the detections of methotrexate (MTX), rutin (RT), quercetin (QCT) and temperature were achieved by a type of highly fluorescent CDs, benefiting from an excitation dependent emission combined with different quenching mechanisms. The highly luminescent CDs were prepared by a simple hydrothermal pyrolysis of citric acid and monoethanolamine at 180 °C for 4 h with a high quantum yield of 46.6%, exhibiting an excitation dependent emission. An approach to the detection of MTX was built by using the CDs as a sensor under excitation at 370 nm based on the synergistic action of inner filter effect (IFE) and photo induced electron transfer (PET). Although the absorption spectra of RT and QCT both centered at 350 nm are entirely overlapped, the maximum absorption of QCT can be moved from 350 to 430 nm since it forms a coordination complex with Cu2+ under acidic condition. Assisted by Cu2+, detections of individual RT and QCT were therefore achieved by the CDs via IFE under excitation at 350 and 430 nm, respectively. Moreover, a nanosensor for temperature sensing was also fabricated according to temperature caused aggregation of the CDs and aggregation induced quenching effect.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Multi-mode sensing
Methotrexate
Rutin
Quercetin
Temperature

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detections of MTX, RT and QCT
Synthesis and characterization of the CDs
MTX sensor based on the CDs
RT and QCT sensors based on the CDs
Detections of MTX, RT and QCT in real samples using the CDs based sensor
The CDs as a temperature sensor

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A schematic illustration for the formation of the CDs synthesized by CA and MEA.
(a) TEM image and particle size distribution (insert), (b) XRD pattern, (c) FT-IR spectrum, and (d) Zeta potential distribution of the CDs.

(a) UV–vis absorption spectrum and fluorescence spectra (excitation wavelengths as indicated with 20 nm increments) of the CDs. (b) The effect of pH on the fluorescence intensity of the CDs at the emission wavelength of 460 nm (λex = 370 nm). Normalized fluorescence intensities of the CDs at 460 nm under λex = 370 nm in the presence of various metal ions (c) in HAc-NaAc buffer solution (0.1 mol L−1, pH 5.0) and (d) in Tris-HCl buffer solution (0.1 mol L−1, pH 7.0) with all the concentrations of the metal ions being 100 μM.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of the CDs in Tris-HCl buffer solution (0.1 mol L−1, pH 7.0) upon addition of various concentrations of MTX (from top to bottom: 0–450 μM; λex = 370 nm and λem = 460 nm). (b) Normalized fluorescence intensities of the CDs at 460 nm under λex = 370 nm in Tris-HCl buffer solution (0.1 mol L−1, pH 7.0) in the presence of various chemical compounds at the concentration of 50 μM. (c) The linear relationships between (F0-F)/F0 and MTX concentration (0–5 and 5–50 μM). (d) UV–vis absorption spectrum of MTX, excitation spectra of the CDs centered at 370 and 430 nm and emission spectra centered at 460 and 490 nm.

(a) UV–vis absorption spectra of QCT, RT, the mixture of Cu2+ and QCT, and the mixture of Cu2+ and RT, the excitation spectra of the CDs center at 350 and 430 nm and the emission spectra centered at 450 and 490 nm. (b) Normalized fluorescence intensities of the CDs solutions (0.5 mg mL−1) containing Cu2+ (100 μM) in HAc-NaAc buffer solution (0.1 mol L−1, pH 5.0) at 450 nm under λex = 350 nm in the presence of various chemical compounds at the concentration of 50 μM (red) and at 490 nm under λex = 430 nm in the presence of various chemical compounds at the concentration of 10 μM (blue). Fluorescence spectra of the CDs solutions (0.5 mg mL−1) containing Cu2+ (100 μM) in HAc-NaAc buffer solution (0.1 mol L−1, pH 5.0) upon addition of various concentrations of (c) RT (from top to bottom: 0–450 μM, λex = 350 nm) and (d) QCT (from top to bottom: 0–450 μM, λex = 430 nm). The insets are the linear relationships between (F0-F)/F0 and RT/QCT concentrations. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) Fluorescence spectra of the CDs (0.5 mg mL−1) at various temperatures in the range of 20–90 °C (top to bottom) at 460 nm under λex = 370 nm. The inset is the linear relationship between fluorescence intensity and temperature. (b) Fluorescence intensities of the CDs at the temperatures switching between 30 °C and 70 °C. (c) and (d) TEM images of the CDs in aqueous solution at 50 °C and 90 °C, respectively. The insets are the size distributions of the CDs at 50 °C and 90 °C.
A comparison between the present method and the previously reported methods for the detections of MTX, RT and QCT.
Results for the detections of the spiked MTX, QCT and RT in the samples of human urine and human blood (the concentration of the spiked Cu2+ in the samples is 100 μM).

head.ce:title: Excitation dependent emission combined with different quenching manners supports carbon dots to achieve multi-mode sensing

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemical and Pharmaceutical Sciences, Qingdao Agricultural University, Qingdao, 266109, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemical and Pharmaceutical Sciences

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this paper, multi-mode sensing for the detections of methotrexate (MTX), rutin (RT), quercetin (QCT) and temperature were achieved by a type of highly fluorescent CDs, benefiting from an excitation dependent emission combined with different quenching mechanisms. The highly luminescent CDs were prepared by a simple hydrothermal pyrolysis of citric acid and monoethanolamine at 180 °C for 4 h with a high quantum yield of 46.6%, exhibiting an excitation dependent emission. An approach to the detection of MTX was built by using the CDs as a sensor under excitation at 370 nm based on the synergistic action of inner filter effect (IFE) and photo induced electron transfer (PET). Although the absorption spectra of RT and QCT both centered at 350 nm are entirely overlapped, the maximum absorption of QCT can be moved from 350 to 430 nm since it forms a coordination complex with Cu2+ under acidic condition. Assisted by Cu2+, detections of individual RT and QCT were therefore achieved by the CDs via IFE under excitation at 350 and 430 nm, respectively. Moreover, a nanosensor for temperature sensing was also fabricated according to temperature caused aggregation of the CDs and aggregation induced quenching effect.

ce:section.ce:para: Multi-mode sensing achieved by one platform with high selectivity, sensitivity and accuracy is considerably attractive for analysts because of functions integration, operation facility, labor saving and low cost [1]. Recently, CDs have been proven to be such excellent platform for multi-mode sensing. For example, triple-mode sensing for metal ions by one CD-based sensor had been reported by our group, in which the selectivity of the CDs capped with COO− can be switched by different buffer solutions, and the CD-based sensor was successfully applied to the detections of individual Fe3+, Hg2+ and Pb2+ ions in mineral water and tap water samples via photo induced electron transfer (PET) mechanism [2]. Another type of triple-mode sensing for Fe3+, Cr(VI) and AA in aqueous solution with high sensitivity and selectivity using one CD-based sensor via different mechanisms was also described by our group [3]. The CDs with dual-emission prepared via one-step thermal decomposition of AA and alcohol had been performed as a highly efficient and sensitive triple-mode sensor for the selective detection of Fe3+, Bi3+ and Al3+ [4]. However, these reported multi-mode sensing platforms based on CDs were all involved in the participations of metal ions under the fixed excitation wavelengths.

Excitation dependent emission is a typical property belonging to most of the reported CDs, that is, emission is tunable under excitation at different wavelengths [5,6]. Therefore, multi-mode sensing with high selectivity and sensitivity can be more profitably achieved by selecting the optimally matched emission or excitation wavelengths. On the other hand, fluorescent responses of CDs to anlytes have been reported commonly in different manners, such as PET [7], inner filter effect (IFE) [8], aggregation induced emission quenching (AEQ) [9]. Generally, PET is the manner of fluorescent responses of CDs to transition metal ions because these metal ions can form coordination complexes with the dots through functional groups on their surface and there are unfilled orbitals in these metal ions for the transferred electrons [10]. IFE refers to the absorption of the excitation or emission light of CDs by absorbers (analytes) in the detection system [11]. And AEQ is related to the aggregation of CDs caused by solvent, temperature, and metal ions [12–14]. Theoretically, excitation dependent emission combined with different response manners is beneficial for CDs to achieve multi-mode sensing functions.

Commonly, CDs can be prepared with easily available and cheap precursors by facile methods, including electrochemical oxidation [15], combustion/thermal [16], microwave heating [17], chemical oxidation [18], hydrothermal carbonization [19], and pyrolysis [20]. Therefore, the multi-mode sensing platforms based on CDs can be constructed considerably cost-effectively. In addition, due to their favorable biocompatibility and low toxicity [21], the CDs based sensing platforms are favorable for biological and medical areas [22].

In this article, we intend to fabricate a multi-mode sensing platform based on CDs for the detections of three drugs, methotrexate (MTX), rutin (RT) and quercetin (QCT), with the expectation of cutting the cost of medical diagnosis. Among these drugs, MTX is an antimetabolite widely applied in the treatment of several cancers at large dose, and in the treatment of rheumatoid arthritis, juvenile idiopathic arthritis and other diseases at low dose [23]. The principal action mechanism of MTX is its competitive inhibition of dihydrofolate reductase, which interferes with the process of DNA synthesis and thus with tissue cell reproduction. With careful dosage control, methotrexate may impair malignant growth without irreversible damage to normal tissues [24]. Therefore, it is necessary to develop a method with selectivity, sensitivity and low cost for monitoring its concentration level in blood and urine.

RT (quercetin-3-ehamnosylglucoside) and QCT (3ʹ,3,4ʹ,5,7-pentahydroxyflavone) belong to the large group of naturally occurring flavonoid compounds found in plants [25]. Lots of studies have revealed their various beneficial effects on human health, such as anti-flammatory, antioxidant, antiviral, antiarrhythmia, anti-tumor, anticancer and expanding blood vessel [26,27]. Monitoring the levels of RT and QCT in blood and urine is interesting for efficacy investigating, dosing schedule, different individual responses, and adverse drug reactions, which requires rapid and sensitive analytical methods. But it is difficult for the detections of individual RT and QCT using optical methods because they have the same part of quercetin in their molecules, resulting in entirely overlapped absorption spectra [28,29].

In this work, the highly luminescent CDs were prepared by a simple hydrothermal pyrolysis of citric acid and monoethanolamine with a high quantum yield, exhibiting an excitation dependent emission. An approach to the detection of MTX was built using the CDs as a sensor under the excitation at 370 nm based on the synergistic action of IFE and PET. Assisted by the formation of coordination complex between QCT and Cu2+ under acidic condition, and the maximum absorption of QCT being moved from 350 to 430 nm, a method for the detections of individual RT and QCT using the CDs as a sensor was fabricated via IFE supported by excitation dependent emission. Moreover, a nanosensor for temperature measurement was also constructed on the basis of temperature increase induced aggregation and the aggregation induced quenching effect of the CDs.

AgNO3, CaCl2, NiCl2, CoCl2, CuCl2, Fe(NO3)3, Hg(Ac)2, MgCl2, MnSO4, Pb(NO3)2, AlCl3, BaCl2, CrCl3, NaCl, LiNO3, KCl, and Zn(NO3)2 were purchased from Damao Chemical Corp (Tianjin, China). Citric acid, monoethanolamine (MEA), sodium acetate trihydrate, Tris, methotrexate (MTX), glutathione (GSH), melamine (MA), L-phenylalanine (Phe), L-tyrosine (Tyr), tryptophan (Trp), histidine (His), lysine (Lys), casein, p-Phenylenediamine (PPD), m-Phenylenediamine (MPD), o-diaminobenzene (OPD), dopamine (DA), rutin (RT), fructose (Fru), bovine albumin (BSA), pyrogallic acid (Pya), phloroglucinol (Pae), ascorbic acid (AA) and quercetin (QCT) were obtained from Aladin Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All chemicals were used without any further purification. The ultrapure water used throughout all the experiments was purified through Water Purifier Nanopure water system (18.3 MΩ cm).

The morphology and the size of CDs were analyzed using the transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Hitachi H-7700, operated at 80 kV). UV–vis absorption spectra collections were performed by the use of a TU-1991 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Pgeneral, China). Fluorescent spectra were collected by a fluorescence spectrometer F-4600 (Hitachi, Japan). The X-ray diffraction (XRD) of the CDs was recorded using a D8 ADVANCE X-ray diffractometer (Bruker AXS, German) with Cu-Kα radiation (40 kV, 40 mA, λ = 1.5418 Å) at a scanning rate of 1° min−1 in the range from 10° to 80°. The Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) spectrum of the CDs were scanned using a FT-IR200 spectrometer (Thermo, America) with KBr pellets technique, over the range 500–4000 cm−1. The quantum yields (QYs) of the prepared CDs were determined using quinine sulfate in 0.1 mol L−1 H2SO4 (literature QY: 54.6%) as the standard sample by comparing the integrated fluorescence intensities (excitation at 370 nm) and absorbance values at 370 nm of the CDs aqueous solutions with those of quinine sulfate [2].

The CDs were synthesized by a facile hydrothermal method. Briefly, 25 mL of monoethanolamine (MEA) solution containing 2 g of citric acid (CA) was sealed in a 50 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and maintained at 180 °C for 4 h. After that, the solution naturally cooled to room temperature. MEA was removed from the obtained CDs dispersion using a rotary evaporator at 100 °C under reduced pressure and subsequently the CDs powders were obtained by dring in a vacuum oven.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detections of MTX, RT and QCT
ce:section.ce:para: MTX was added into Tris-HCl buffer solution (0.1 mol L−1, pH 7) of the CDs (0.5 mg mL−1) at various concentrations, then their fluorescence spectra were recorded after being shaken for 30 min. For the detections of RT and QCT, the CDs/Cu2+ system was established by mixing the CDs (0.5 mg mL−1) and Cu2+ (0.10 mM) in HAc-NaAc buffer solution (0.1 mol L−1, pH 5.0), thereafter, RT and QCT were individually added into CDs/Cu2+ system at different concentrations, then the fluorescence spectra were measured under excitations at 350 and 430 nm. The fluorescence intensities at 450, 460 and 490 nm were used to fabricate the linear relationships with the concentrations of MTX, RT and QCT.

The human urine samples were treated by 0.45 μm filter membrane, and then diluted for 100 times. And the human blood samples were centrifuged at 14000 rpm for 15 min, treated by 0.45 μm filter membrane, then diluted for 100 times. The treated urine and blood samples were spiked with MTX, RT and QCT at different concentrations, respectively. Finally, their recoveries were performed by the proposed methods.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of the CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The crystalline of the CDs was identified by XRD pattern. As depicted in Fig. 1b, a diffraction broad peak centered at 27.6° was observed, which is corresponding to an interlayer spacing of 0.32 nm, close to the (002) diffraction facets of graphitic carbon [14,30].

FTIR was used to recognize the functional groups on the surface of the CDs (Fig. 1c). An absorption band at 3349 cm−1 in the spectrum of the CDs was assigned to the OH, and the bands at 3085, 2959 and 2891 cm−1 were attributed to CH, CH3 and CH [10]. The absorption peaks at 1664, 1550 and 1265 cm−1 were ascribed to the vibrations of CO, NH and CN. The absorption peak at 1396 cm−1 belonged to the deformation vibration of OH on the surface and the band at 1057 cm−1 was assigned to the stretching vibration of CO [31]. Fig. 1d is the zeta potential distribution of the CDs, exhibiting a very big peak centered at −27.3 eV and a small peak centered at +42.6 eV, which suggests that most of the CDs are negatively charged due to the surface dominated by COOH and a small part of the CDs are positively charged due to the surface dominated by amine. Combined the results of FTIR and zeta potential, there were OH, amine, COOH and amide groups on the surface of the CDs.

There are a shoulder peak at 250 nm and a broad band at 350 nm in the UV–vis absorption spectrum of the CDs (Fig. 2 a), attributed to the π-π* transition of the sp2 domain and the n-π* transition of the CO group, respectively [7,15].

The CDs exhibited an excitation-dependent fluorescence emission, which is a typical optical property of most of the reported CDs (see Fig. 2a). The fluorescence intensity firstly increased then decreased, and the emission peak was red-shifted as the excitation wavelength increased from 330 to 450 nm. And the maximum emission peaked at 460 nm under excitation at 370 nm. It is generally believed that the surface states mainly control the fluorescence of the CDs [15]. The Quantum yield of the CDs under the excitation at 370 nm was measured to be 46.6% using quinine sulfate in 0.1 mol L−1 H2SO4 (literature QY: 54.6%) as reference [2].

The effect of pH on the fluorescence property of the CDs was investigated by measuring the emission intensity at 460 nm under excitation at 370 nm in the solutions at different pH values. As shown in Fig. 2b, the fluorescence intensity of the CDs slightly increased with an increase in pH ranging from 3.0 to 7.0 (HAc-NaAc buffer), and showed pH independence in the range from 7.0 to 12.0 (Tris-HCl buffer).
Also metal ions exerted negligible effects on the fluorescence of the CDs whatever in HAc-NaAc buffer solution (pH = 5, Fig. 2c) or in Tris-HCl buffer solution (pH = 7, Fig. 2d).

ce:section.ce:section-title: MTX sensor based on the CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Interestingly, the fluorescence of the CDs could be quenched upon addition of MTX and was sensitive to its concentration variation (see Fig. 3 a). As a result, the CDs have a potential to be developed as a fluorescence sensor for the label-free detection of MTX. Subsequently, the selectivity of the CDs based sensor for the determination of MTX was evaluated by measuring the fluorescence intensity of the CDs in the presence of potential interfering substances (50 μM), including GSH, MA, Phe, Tyr, Trp, Glc, Casein, PPD, OPD, MPD, DA, and UA. As shown in Fig. 3b, except that only MTX can significantly quench the fluorescence of the CDs, the other substances exerted negligible effects on the fluorescence intensity of the CDs. Therefore, the CDs based sensor showed high selectivity to MTX. Since the fluorescence of the CDs did not suffer from pH variation in Tris-HCl buffer solution in the range of 7.0–12.0, Tris-HCl at pH 7.0 was chosen as the detection buffer. Under the optimized condition, there were two excellent linear relationships between the (F0−F)/F0 and the concentration of MTX in the ranges from 0 to 5 μM and 5 to 50 μM (see Fig. 3c), with two calibration equations as (F0−F)/F0 = 0.0254c − 0.0057 (R2 = 0.9937) and (F0−F)/F0 = 0.0103c + 0.0744 (R2 = 0.9955), respectively. Herein, F0 and F are the fluorescence intensities of the CDs at 460 nm in the absence and presence of MTX, and c (mol L−1) represents the concentration of MTX. The limit of detection (LOD) for MTX was evaluated to be 0.24 μM based on three times the standard deviation rule (LOD = 3Sd/s).

The mechanism of the quenching effect of MTX on the CDs was also investigated as shown in Fig. 3d. The absorption spectrum of MTX partially overlapped with the excitation spectrum of the CDs in the range of 300–450 nm. Therefore there is an IFE effect between MTX and the CDs under the excitation of 370 nm. However, when the excitation wavelength moved to 430 nm where there is no IFE of MTX on the CDs, quenching effect was still observed, which might result from PET from the CDs to MTX because of their conjugate formation via hydrogen bond [32]. Therefore, fluorescence quenching of the CDs by MTX under excitation at 370 nm was the synergistic effect of IFE and PET. This may be the reason why there were two linear relationships in the concentration range from 0 to 50 μM. At low concentration, the synergistic effect of IFE and PET resulted in higher quenching efficiency and sensitivity with a larger slope of the fitted line, while at high concentration, IFE might become the main quenching factor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: RT and QCT sensors based on the CDs
ce:section.ce:para: RT and QCT individually have an absorption band centered at 350 nm in their absorption spectra, and the two bands were entirely overlapped with each other, and also with the excitation spectra of the CDs. Therefore, the CDs can respond to both RT and QCT in fluorescence way via IFE (Fig. 4 a). However, it is difficult to directly use the CDs as a label free fluorescent sensor for the detections of individual RT and QCT via IFE because of their mutual interference. Instead, in HAc-NaAc buffer solution (pH = 5.0), QCT can form a coordination complex with Cu2+ by producing a new chelating ring through the 3OH and 4oxo groups, resulting in a new absorption peak at 430 nm [33]. Whereas, RT cannot form a stable coordination complex with Cu2+ in HAc-NaAc buffer solution (pH = 5) [34], as a result, the absorption peak did not change upon addition of Cu2+. Since the emission of the CDs is excitation dependent, RT and the Cu2+ complex of QCT can separately exert efficient IFE on the CDs at the excitation wavelengths of 350 and 430 nm. Eventually, assisted by Cu2+, the CDs have the possibility to be a label free fluorescent sensor for the detections of individual RT and QCT via IFE.

Subsequently, the selectivities of the CDs to RT under excitation at 350 nm and to QCT under excitation at 430 nm were investigated by collecting the fluorescence spectra in the presence of Cu2+ and the potential interferences, including RT, Gls, Fru, BSA, Pae, Pya, Phe, Trp, His, Lys, AA, UA, DA, GSH and QCT under the same measurement condition. As shown in Fig. 4b, under excitation at 350 nm, RT exerted significant quenching effect on the CDs, with slight interference from the QCT-Cu2+ complex. The other substances exhibited negligible effect on the fluorescence intensity of the CDs. Therefore, assisted by Cu2+, the CDs showed high selectivity to RT under excitation at 350 nm, even though QCT coexisted in the solution. On the other hand, under excitation at 430 nm and in the presence of Cu2+, all the substances including RT nearly did not affect the fluorescence intensity of the CDs. It suggests Cu2+ can assist the CDs to selectively distinguish RT and QCT in the presence of other biomolecules under different excitations. The CDs, therefore, can be developed as a sensor for selective detection of RT and QCT in the biological samples.

The sensitivities to RT and QCT in the presence of Cu2+ were further investigated by recording the fluorescence spectra of the CDs solutions containing RT and QCT at various concentrations under excitations at 350 and 430 nm, respectively. As shown in Fig. 4c and d, the fluorescence intensities of the CDs under excitations at 350 and 430 nm decreased upon additions of RT and QCT. Fig. 4c and d present the fitted curves of (F0−F)/F0 versus the concentrations of RT and QCT under excitations at 350 and 430 nm, respectively. A good linear correlation (R2 = 0.9976) was obtained in the concentration range from 1 to 50 μM for RT under excitation at 350 nm. The linear regression equation was calibrated as (F0−F)/F0 = 0.0526 + 0.0105c (c represents the concentration of RT), and the LOD was estimated to be 2.8 μM.

As for QCT detection under excitation at 430 nm, there was a good linear relationship between (F0−F)/F0 and the concentration in the range from 0 to 10 μM with a linear regression equation of (F0−F)/F0 = 0.0039 + 0.0359c (R2 = 0.9973), and the LOD was estimated to be 0.35 μM. Thus, the CDs have a potential to be developed as a fluorescent sensor for the selective detection of RT under the excitation at 350 nm and QCT under the excitation at 430 nm.

A comparison between the as-proposed method and the previously reported methods was performed as illustrated in Table 1 . Even though the most attractive performance of the proposed method is achieving multi-mode sensing for MTX, RT and QCT, the LOD and linear range of the present method using one CDs-based sensor for the detections of three components are comparable or even better than those the other methods previously reported for single one [23–27,35–37].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detections of MTX, RT and QCT in real samples using the CDs based sensor
ce:section.ce:para: Due to the high sensitivity and selectivity, the CDs can be directly applied for the detections of MTX, and RT/QCT assisted by Cu2+ in real samples. In this work, human urine and human blood samples were applied as the real samples to assess the feasibility of the CDs for the detections of MTX, RT and QCT. All the samples were pretreated by 0.45 μM Supor filters to remove any suspension, and then spiked with MTX, RT and QCT, respectively. Finally, the spiked MTX, RT and QCT were recovered by the proposed method. As presented in Table 2 , the recoveries for the selective detections of MTX were in the range of 96.37%–103.52%, RT in the range of 97.82%–103.01%, and QCT in the range of 96.95%–104.27%. Although the components of the real samples are very complicated and they may interfere with the measurements, the spiked MTX, RT and QCT can be recovered from these samples with accuracy, which indicates that the proposed methods are of high selectivity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The CDs as a temperature sensor
ce:section.ce:para: More interestingly, the CDs was very sensitive to the temperature variation (see Fig. 5 ). That is, the fluorescence intensity decreased promptly as the temperature increased. Approximate 45% in fluorescence intensity was quenched when the temperature of the CDs solution was enhanced from 20 to 90 °C. As shown in Fig. 5a, there was a good linear relationship between fluorescence intensity of the CDs and temperature, fitted as a function of F(T) = 486.76 − 2.84T with correlation coefficient being 0.9941, where T and F(T) represent temperature and fluorescence intensity at 460 nm under excitation at 370 nm. The fluorescence intensity of the CDs remained unchanged in a buffer solution after four cycles of temperature switch between 70 °C to 30 °C (Fig. 5b). The reproducibility of the emission intensity clearly demonstrates the excellent reversibility and the robustness of the CDs. Therefore, the CDs have a potential to be developed as a sensitive, robust temperature sensor.

In order to investigate the mechanism of the CDs sensitive to temperature, TEM was used to characterize the CDs at different temperatures. The average diameter of CDs at room temperature was only 13.72 nm (Fig. 1a), while the average diameter of CDs at 50 °C was 57.63 nm (Fig. 5c). Furthermore, the average diameter of CDs reached 97.86 nm as temperature arrived at 90 °C (Fig. 5d). Therefore, it can be deduced that temperature increase caused aggregation of the CDs, which induced fluorescence quenching [38–40].

In this work, the highly luminescent CDs with an excitation dependent emission can be prepared by a simple hydrothermal pyrolysis of citric acid and monoethanolamine. Under excitation at 370 nm, a detection method for MTX using the CDs based sensor has been built via synergistic action of IFE and PET. Under excitations at 350 and 430 nm, an approach to the detections of individual RT and QCT can also be fabricated via IFE assisted by Cu2+. The three drugs were recovered from the human urine and blood samples with high accuracy, suggesting the feasibility of the proposed method applied in real samples. Moreover, a nanosensor for temperature measurement can be constructed based on the CDs as well via the mechanism of temperature increase induced aggregation and aggregation induced quenching effect. In conclusion, excitation dependent emission combined with different quenching mechanisms supports CDs to achieve multi-mode sensing.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 31572181), Key Research and Development Project of Shandong Province, China (No. 2016GSF116007), Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province, China (No. ZR2014CM012).

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Chun Li was born in Zhaodong City, Heilongjiang Province, China. He received his BSc from Kunming University of Science and Technology in 2014. He joined Prof. Jinping Wang’s group at Qingdao Agricultural University as a graduate student in 2015. His current research focuses on synthesis of carbon dots and their applications in biosensors and bioimaging.

Weijian Liu received her BSc in Material Chemistry from Qingdao Agricultural University in 2015. She is currently a graduate student at Qingdao Agricultural University, majoring in applied chemistry. Her research interests are primarily in preparation of fluorescent probes, and their applications in analytical chemistry.

Xiaobo Sun received his Ph.D. from Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences, in 2005. Now she is an Associate Professor in College of Chemistry and Pharmaceutical sciences at Qingdao Agricultural University, China. Her research interests are primarily in analytical chemistry and separation science.

Wei Pan was born in Shandong, China, in 1979. He received his MSc from Sichuan University in 2006. Then he joined Qingdao Agricultural University in Sep. 2006 as an experimentalist. His research interests focus on functional nanomaterials and devices.

Guifeng Yu received her MSc from Sichuan University in 2005 and her Ph.D from Qingdao University in 2016. Her current scientific interests are focused on electrospinning, functional nanomaterials and their applications in nanodevices.

Jinping Wang received his Ph.D. from Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences, in 2004. He received postdoctoral training at Waseda University in 2007–2009, and worked as a visiting scholar at Clemson University in 2012–2013. Now he is a Full Professor in College of Chemistry and Pharmaceutical sciences at Qingdao Agricultural University, China. His research interests are primarily in preparation of carbon-based nanomaterials, and their bioimaging and sensing applications.
